query: pathophysiology of myocardial oxygen supply and demand imbalance

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Myocardial Oxygen Demand -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A mismatch between myocardial oxygen supply and demand can result in myocardial ischemia or infarct. Unfortunately, infarct results in irreversible damage to the myocardium. However, ischemia and potential causes of mismatch can be controlled through a variety of pharmaceutical agents that include nitrates, beta-adrenergic-receptor blockers, and calcium-channel blockers. Each of these medication classes increases myocardial oxygen supply and decrease myocardial oxygen demand to varying degrees [1] [7] .

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Myocardial Oxygen Demand -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Heart rate is thought to be the most important factor affecting myocardial oxygen demand. With an increased heart rate, the myocardium must work harder to complete the cardiac cycle more efficiently. With a shortened cardiac cycle, the time spent in diastole decreases. Because diastole ends prematurely, the amount of blood that normally fills the ventricles decreases, and oxygen-saturated hemoglobin is not allowed to reach the subendocardium. Under optimal conditions, myocardial oxygen demand will equal myocardial oxygen supply; however, when there is structural damage from a plaque that impedes flow, there can be a mismatch between supply and demand that results in ischemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Coronary Circulation -- Issues of Concern -- Regulation
retrieved_docs.content: Myocardial oxygen demand can increase several-fold depending on ventricular rate, contractility, and pressures. Due to the high baseline oxygen consumption of the myocardium, increased oxygen extraction provides only a limited buffer capacity. The majority of this demand must be met by increased coronary flow, the mechanisms of which are only partially understood. Current evidence suggests a multifactorial model of coronary regulation. Downstream metabolites of oxygen consumption, such as carbon dioxide, are thought to be the primary determinant of coronary flow under physiologic conditions at rest. Meanwhile, localized hypoxia, along with the resultant release of vasodilatory substances, likely contributes to coronary vasodilation during various physiologic and pathophysiologic states of mismatched oxygen supply and demand.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Myocardial Oxygen Demand -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A rise in myocardial oxygen demand can become clinically significant if it exceeds myocardial oxygen supply. This can occur during the later stages of coronary artery disease (CAD). From years of poorly controlled hyperlipidemia, a patient can develop atherosclerotic plaques in the major arteries that supply blood to the heart. Once the integrity of the vasculature has been compromised, plaques can develop and begin to shorten the diameter of the coronary arteries [1] .

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Myocardial Oxygen Demand -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Ventricular wall tension is based on the thickness of the ventricular myocardium. The law of Laplace states that ventricular wall tension is proportional to ventricular radius and intraventricular pressure. Patients with coronary artery disease will have decreased blood and oxygen supply to the myocardium. In this case, contractility will be diminished. Because the ventricle can no longer achieve adequate contraction, the end-systolic volume will increase. The excess blood remaining in the ventricle will cause expansion of the ventricular radius and intraventricular pressure resulting in higher wall tension.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Myocardial Oxygen Demand -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: If the ECG is unremarkable, cardiologists may choose to perform an exercise stress test. In a controlled environment, cardiologists can monitor the patient’s blood pressure, oxygen saturation, and electrical activity of the heart. By performing an exercise, the patient is causing the heart to increase its rate and contractility thus elevating the myocardial oxygen demand. If the vessels are atherosclerotic, the heart will not be able to adapt to the changes in demand thus there will be a mismatch between supply and demand which will be represented accordingly on the ECG [6] [4] .

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Myocardial Oxygen Demand -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrates cause a relaxation in the vascular smooth muscle which causes dilatation of the coronary arteries and systemic venous circulation. When the vessels carrying de-oxygenated blood back to the heart are dilated causing the venous capacity to increase, there are corresponding decreases in cardiac preload, ventricular volume, and ventricular wall tension during systole. Because of the downward shift in workload required by the heart, myocardial oxygen demand is reduced.  Additionally, through the dilatation of coronary arteries, nitrates also increase myocardial oxygen supply. The increased radius of the vasculature supplying the heart allows blood to bypass potential plaques to provide a continuous supply of blood [8] [9] .

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Myocardial Oxygen Demand -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A person who presents to the emergency department with angina should be evaluated for a mismatch in myocardial oxygen supply and demand. The first test to determine this is a 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) which measures the electrical activity of the heart. The ST segment is representative of the time between ventricular depolarization and ventricular repolarization. If the ST segment is elevated upon arrival, it can be indicative of acute myocardial infarction; however, if there is a depressed ST segment, it can be representative of acute ischemia [2] [4] .

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Coronary Circulation -- Issues of Concern -- Regulation
retrieved_docs.content: At the most basic level, local hypoxemia and hypercarbia have shown to correlate with coronary vasodilation. Measurements of coronary venous pO2 and pCO2, however, show little, if any, change during states of physiologically increased demand (i.e., exercise.) [4] This situation suggests that alternative factors must contribute to coronary regulation under normal conditions that prevent hypoxemia and hypercarbia. Indeed, multiple studies have demonstrated that the concentrations of both oxygen and carbon dioxide are insufficient in explaining the majority of the total extent of coronary vasodilation in response to increased oxygen demand. While it is likely that localized hypoxemia and hypercarbia have a role in coronary regulation during pathophysiologic states, it is not yet clear whether an intermediary molecule is involved in the process.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Coronary Circulation -- Issues of Concern -- Regulation
retrieved_docs.content: In the 1960s, adenosine was a proposed possible metabolite responsible for triggering coronary vasodilation. The hypothesis was that decreased oxygen tension stimulated the release of adenosine due to the consumption and degradation of adenosine triphosphate (ATP). The thinking was that adenosine then acted on vascular smooth muscle adenosine receptors to result in increased coronary flow. Decades of research in the interim, however, have been unable to demonstrate the role of adenosine in physiologic coronary vasodilation conclusively. This disconnect is perhaps due to the ability of myocyte ATP production to keep pace with consumption when oxygen demand is adequately met. In contrast, adenosine has proven to play a role in coronary regulation during times of ischemia. When normal coronary vasodilation is insufficient, ischemic cardiac tissue releases adenosine in large quantities, resulting in local hyperemia. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Myocardial Oxygen Demand -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Next, the second portion of the nuclear stress test can begin. The accumulation of the nuclear isotope in the vessels allows clear images of the coronary arteries and surrounding vasculature to be obtained. Currently, the standard model includes gathering images from seventeen segments: 16 short-axis views and the long-axis view of the apical segment. Cardiologists grade each view using a scale range of zero to four; 0 = normal, 1 = mildly abnormal, 2 = moderately abnormal, 3 = severely abnormal, 4 = absent. The summed perfusion score (SPS) is the total score including images from rest and stress, the summed resting score (SRS) represents the severity of myocardial infarction, and the summed stress score (SSS) reflects the extent of myocardial ischemia and infarction. To determine the severity of ischemia alone, the summed difference score (SDS) must be calculated by subtracting the SRS from the SSS [6] [4] .

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Myocardial Oxygen Demand -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood and flow through the coronary arteries regulate myocardial oxygen supply. Oxygen-carrying capacity can be decreased in several conditions that include either decreased red blood cell concentration or decreased oxygen saturation of hemoglobin. For example, anemia is a lower than average number of healthy red blood cells. Even though the red blood cells may be fully saturated with oxygen, there are not enough of them to supply an adequate amount of oxygen to the muscle. Usually, it is caused by a nutritional deficiency in iron, vitamin B12, or folate which is easily correctable. However, there can be more devastating causes such as thalassemia, sickle cell anemia, and various inherent enzyme deficiencies that are more difficult to treat [1] [3] .

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Coronary Circulation -- Issues of Concern -- Regulation
retrieved_docs.content: At rest, approximately 60% to 70% of oxygen is extracted from blood in the coronary arteries. [1] This degree of oxygen extraction is a testament to the high metabolic activity of the myocardium. It also highlights the importance of increasing overall coronary flow during times of increased myocardial oxygen demand.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Myocardial Oxygen Demand -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, if patients have a contraindication to an exercise stress ECG, they can undergo myocardial perfusion imaging (MPI). Myocardial perfusion imaging can be used to examine the myocardium for deficits in myocardial oxygen supply due to atherosclerotic plaques from coronary artery disease. First, the patients are given an adenosine analog and a nuclear isotope which are both injected via an intravenous catheter. The adenosine analog stresses the heart and increases myocardial oxygen demand. The patient is monitored for changes in oxygen saturation, blood pressure, heart rate, and rhythm [4] .

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiogenic Shock -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of cardiogenic shock is complex and not fully understood. Ischemia to the myocardium causes derangement to both systolic and diastolic left ventricular function, resulting in a profound depression of myocardial contractility. This, in turn, leads to a potentially catastrophic and vicious spiral of reduced cardiac output and low blood pressure, perpetuating further coronary ischemia and impairment of contractility. Several physiologic compensatory processes ensue. These include: The activation of the sympathetic system leading to peripheral vasoconstriction may improve coronary perfusion at the cost of increased afterload, and Tachycardia increases myocardial oxygen demand and subsequently worsens myocardial ischemia.

Recognizing that these diverse clinical presentations share the same myocardial and conduction‑system substrate and acute infarct morphology is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely therapeutic intervention, and preservation of both contractile function and electrical stability.

retrieved_docs.content: artery, intermediate artery). Despite these variations in presentation and vascular involvement, the pathological finding site for all of them is the myocardial tissue, underscoring that myocardial injury is the central feature of acute coronary syndromes regardless of the precise ECG morphology or culprit vessel. Recognizing the specific type of infarction and its associated arterial occlusion aids clinicians in tailoring reperfusion strategies and optimizing patient care while acknowledging that the underlying myocardial damage remains the pivotal concern.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2726
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب قلبي, تصلب الشريان التاجي, فشل قلبي احتقاني, التهاب النسيج الخلوي, and قسطرة قلبية are closely interconnected clinical entities. اضطراب قلبي is clinically associated with تصلب الشريان التاجي, indicating that structural abnormalities of the coronary arteries often coexist with or contribute to broader cardiac dysfunction. تصلب الشريان التاجي is further linked to فشل قلبي احتقاني, reflecting that progressive atherosclerotic narrowing of the coronary vessels can lead to impaired myocardial perfusion and subsequent congestive heart failure. قسطرة قلبية is repeatedly noted as a clinical association for اضطراب قلبي, تصلب الشريان التاجي, فشل قلبي احتقاني, and التهاب النسيج الخلوي, underscoring its central role as a diagnostic and interventional tool for evaluating and managing these conditions. The association of التهاب النسيج الخلوي with قسطرة قلبية suggests that inflammatory processes of the cardiac tissue are either assessed during catheter‑based procedures or may represent a complication requiring catheter‑based intervention. Together, these relationships highlight the overlapping pathophysiology of cardiac disorders, coronary artery disease, heart failure, and inflammatory cardiac conditions, and emphasize the pivotal role of قسطرة قلبية in their assessment and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2819
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ) is closely related and may be considered synonymous with coronary artery occlusion (انسداد الشريان التاجي). It is also mapped to coronary artery embolism (انصمام الشريان التاجي), indicating that these terms describe similar pathological events in which a blockage of a coronary artery—whether by occlusion or embolic material—leads to sudden injury of the heart muscle. Recognizing the interchangeability of these concepts assists clinicians in diagnosing and managing the acute coronary syndrome that results from compromised coronary blood flow.

Together, these relationships delineate a network in which various rhythm disorders, their anatomical substrates, therapeutic options, and contraindications are interlinked, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing cardiac conduction and rhythm abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: tissue in the pathophysiology and evaluation of AMI. Morphologically, acute infarct tissue links postoperative acute myocardial infarction and anterior non‑ST‑segment‑elevation myocardial infarction, indicating shared pathological changes across these clinical presentations. Acute subendocardial infarction is classified under “other specified forms of acute ischemic heart disease,” connecting it to a broader group of acute ischemic cardiac conditions. Together, these relationships underscore that coronary artery obstruction at diverse arterial segments is the primary mechanism driving myocardial damage, and that the common histopathology and diagnostic classification of the various AMI phenotypes are essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2896
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac function is a broad physiological concept that includes measures such as معدل ضربات القلب (heart rate). In clinical practice, the evaluation of cardiac function is interpreted specifically in the context of congestive heart failure in patients who are non‑hypertensive, providing important information for diagnosis and management.
